fixSteps.instruction: In the affected game, reduce settings like ray tracing, shadow quality, and resolution. Reduce VRAM usage to below your GPU's VRAM capacity.

fixSteps.title: Clear shader cache
fixSteps.instruction: Delete the shader cache: for NVIDIA, use GeForce Experience or delete files in %localappdata%\NVIDIA\DXCache. For AMD, use Adrenalin > Settings > Graphics > Reset Shader Cache.

fixSteps.title: Disable hardware-accelerated GPU scheduling
fixSteps.instruction: Settings > System > Display > Graphics > Change default graphics settings > Turn off Hardware-accelerated GPU scheduling. Restart.

whenToCallPro: If the error affects all GPU-intensive applications despite driver updates and setting changes, professional GPU diagnostics may be needed.

faq.q: What is the difference between DEVICE_HUNG and DEVICE_REMOVED?
faq.a: DEVICE_HUNG means the GPU stopped responding but is still present. DEVICE_REMOVED means the GPU was reset or disconnected entirely. HUNG is usually less severe.

faq.q: Why does this only happen in certain games?
faq.a: Different games use different shader complexity and GPU features. A driver bug may only manifest with specific shaders or rendering techniques used by certain games.

faq.q: Does disabling GPU scheduling help?
faq.a: Hardware-accelerated GPU scheduling changes how Windows manages GPU work queues. Disabling it reverts to the traditional model which may avoid certain timing issues.

title: Direct3D 11 Initialization or Runtime Error
shortDescription: A Direct3D 11 graphics error occurred, preventing games or GPU-accelerated applications from running.
fullDescription:
Direct3D 11 (D3D11) errors encompass a range of issues with the DirectX 11 graphics API. These include failed device creation (the GPU does not support DX11), feature level mismatches, and runtime errors during rendering. Common error messages include 'D3D11 device creation failed', 'Feature Level 11_0 required', and various HRESULT error codes.

These errors typically occur when running modern games on older GPUs that lack DirectX 11 support, when GPU drivers are outdated or corrupted, or when the DirectX runtime files are missing or damaged.

Resolution involves updating GPU drivers, installing the DirectX End-User Runtime, and verifying GPU hardware compatibility with the application's requirements.

causes.cause: GPU does not support required DirectX feature level

symptoms: Game fails to launch with D3D11 error message
Error about Feature Level 11_0 required
Black screen when launching GPU-accelerated application
Application reports D3D device creation failed

fixSteps.title: Update GPU drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Install the latest GPU drivers from NVIDIA, AMD, or Intel. This ensures maximum DirectX feature support for your hardware.

fixSteps.title: Install DirectX End-User Runtime
fixSteps.instruction: Download and run the DirectX End-User Runtime Web Installer from Microsoft. This installs legacy D3D components that some games require.

fixSteps.title: Check GPU DirectX support
fixSteps.instruction: Press Win+R, type dxdiag, click Yes. Under the Display tab, check Feature Levels. If DX11 is not listed, your GPU does not support it.

fixSteps.title: Force DirectX version in game
fixSteps.instruction: Some games allow forcing a lower DX version via launch options (e.g., -dx10, -force-d3d9) or in their settings file. Check the game's documentation.

whenToCallPro: If your GPU does not support the required DirectX level, the only fix is upgrading to a newer GPU.

faq.q: How do I know if my GPU supports DirectX 11?
faq.a: Run dxdiag (Win+R > dxdiag). Under the Display tab, look at Feature Levels. Most GPUs from 2010 onward support DirectX 11.

faq.q: Is DirectX 11 included with Windows 10/11?
faq.a: The core DirectX 11 runtime is included. However, some games require additional D3DX libraries from the legacy DirectX End-User Runtime package.

faq.q: Can I upgrade DirectX without upgrading my GPU?
faq.a: DirectX support is determined by GPU hardware. You cannot enable DX11 features on a GPU that only supports DX10 in hardware.

title: MSVCP140.dll Not Found Error
shortDescription: The program cannot start because MSVCP140.dll is missing from your computer.
fullDescription:
MSVCP140.dll is a component of the Microsoft Visual C++ 2015-2022 Redistributable. This DLL contains the C++ Standard Library implementation used by applications compiled with Visual Studio 2015 or later. When it is missing, applications that depend on it cannot start.

This error commonly occurs after a fresh Windows installation, when the Visual C++ Redistributable was not installed by the application's installer, or when the DLL was accidentally deleted or corrupted by disk cleanup tools or antivirus software.

The fix is straightforward: install the Microsoft Visual C++ 2015-2022 Redistributable package. Both x86 (32-bit) and x64 (64-bit) versions should be installed to cover all applications.

causes.cause: DLL deleted by cleanup tool or antivirus
Malware replaced or removed the DLL

symptoms: Error dialog: MSVCP140.dll was not found
Application fails to launch with missing DLL error
Multiple applications stop working simultaneously
Error appears after Windows reinstallation

fixSteps.title: Install Visual C++ Redistributable
fixSteps.instruction: Download the Microsoft Visual C++ 2015-2022 Redistributable from Microsoft's website. Install BOTH the x86 and x64 versions. Restart your computer.

fixSteps.title: Repair existing installation
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Settings > Apps > search for 'Microsoft Visual C++ 2015-2022 Redistributable' > click Modify > Repair.

fixSteps.title: Reinstall the affected application
fixSteps.instruction: Uninstall and reinstall the application that is showing the error. Many installers bundle the required redistributable.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC scan
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: sfc /scannow. This can restore system DLLs that were corrupted or deleted.

whenToCallPro: This error is almost always fixable by installing the redistributable. Call a professional only if the DLL keeps disappearing, which may indicate malware.

faq.q: Should I download DLLs from third-party websites?
faq.a: Never. Downloading DLLs from random websites is a major security risk. Always install the official Visual C++ Redistributable from Microsoft.

faq.q: Why do I need both x86 and x64 versions?
faq.a: 32-bit applications need the x86 version and 64-bit applications need the x64 version. Installing both ensures all applications work.

faq.q: Will this fix other missing DLL errors too?
faq.a: The Visual C++ Redistributable includes many DLLs (MSVCP140, VCRUNTIME140, CONCRT140, etc.). Installing it may fix multiple missing DLL errors at once.

title: VCRUNTIME140.dll Not Found Error
shortDescription: The program cannot start because VCRUNTIME140.dll is missing from your computer.
fullDescription:
VCRUNTIME140.dll is part of the Microsoft Visual C++ 2015-2022 Redistributable and provides the C runtime library functions that compiled applications need. It is one of the most commonly missing DLLs on Windows systems because many modern applications depend on it.

The error typically appears when a fresh Windows installation lacks the Visual C++ Redistributable, or when the file is corrupted. Some users encounter VCRUNTIME140_1.dll missing as well, which is an additional component added in later Visual Studio versions.

The solution is identical to other Visual C++ runtime errors: install the official Microsoft Visual C++ 2015-2022 Redistributable package in both x86 and x64 variants.

causes.cause: File removed by aggressive cleanup software
Windows Update removed or corrupted the file

symptoms: Error dialog: VCRUNTIME140.dll was not found
Application exits immediately on launch
Games and creative software refuse to start
Error for VCRUNTIME140_1.dll as well

fixSteps.title: Install Visual C++ Redistributable
fixSteps.instruction: Download Microsoft Visual C++ 2015-2022 Redistributable from Microsoft's official site. Install both x86 and x64 versions, then restart.

fixSteps.title: Repair via Settings
fixSteps.instruction: Settings > Apps > Find 'Microsoft Visual C++ 2015-2022 Redistributable' > Modify > Repair for both x86 and x64.

fixSteps.title: Uninstall and reinstall all VC++ versions
fixSteps.instruction: In Settings > Apps, uninstall ALL Microsoft Visual C++ Redistributable entries. Then reinstall the latest 2015-2022 package (it covers 2015, 2017, 2019, 2022).

fixSteps.title: Run Windows Update
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Settings > Windows Update > Check for updates. Some Visual C++ runtime updates are delivered through Windows Update.

whenToCallPro: This is one of the simplest Windows errors to fix. Professional help is only needed if the redistributable repeatedly fails to install.

faq.q: What is the difference between MSVCP140.dll and VCRUNTIME140.dll?
faq.a: MSVCP140.dll is the C++ Standard Library and VCRUNTIME140.dll is the C Runtime Library. Both are part of the same Visual C++ Redistributable package and installed together.

faq.q: What about VCRUNTIME140_1.dll?
faq.a: VCRUNTIME140_1.dll is an additional component introduced with Visual Studio 2019 updates. It is included in the latest Visual C++ 2015-2022 Redistributable.

faq.q: Do I need older Visual C++ versions too?
faq.a: Some older applications require Visual C++ 2012 or 2013 redistributables. If an application specifically asks for those, install them separately.

title: Universal C Runtime DLL Missing
shortDescription: The program cannot start because api-ms-win-crt-runtime-l1-1-0.dll is missing, indicating the Universal C Runtime is not installed.
fullDescription:
The api-ms-win-crt-runtime-l1-1-0.dll is part of the Universal C Runtime (UCRT), which was introduced with Windows 10 and backported to older versions via Windows Update. This component is required by applications built with Visual Studio 2015 or later.

On Windows 10 and 11, the UCRT is a system component and should always be present. If this error occurs, it usually means the Windows installation is damaged, critical updates are missing, or the system files are corrupted.

On older Windows versions (7, 8, 8.1), installing the KB2999226 update or the Visual C++ 2015-2022 Redistributable resolves the issue.

causes.cause: Windows Update KB2999226 not installed (older Windows)

symptoms: Error: api-ms-win-crt-runtime-l1-1-0.dll is missing
Multiple applications fail to start with this error
Error appears after Windows reinstallation
Visual C++ Redistributable fails to install with this error

fixSteps.title: Run Windows Update
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Settings > Windows Update > Check for updates. Install all pending updates, especially cumulative updates. Restart.

fixSteps.title: Install Visual C++ Redistributable
fixSteps.instruction: Download and install the latest Visual C++ 2015-2022 Redistributable (both x86 and x64) from Microsoft.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC and DISM
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth then sfc /scannow. Restart and check.

fixSteps.title: Install KB2999226 manually
fixSteps.instruction: For Windows 7/8/8.1: Download KB2999226 from the Microsoft Update Catalog and install it manually.

whenToCallPro: If SFC and DISM cannot restore the UCRT and Windows Update fails, the Windows installation may need repair or reset.

faq.q: What is the Universal C Runtime?
faq.a: The UCRT is the system-level C runtime library for Windows. It provides basic C functions like printf, malloc, and file I/O that virtually all applications need.

faq.q: Why is this missing on Windows 10/11?
faq.a: It should not be. If it is missing, system files are corrupted. SFC and DISM repairs should restore it. A Windows repair install may be needed in severe cases.

faq.q: Is this the same as the Visual C++ Redistributable?
faq.a: Related but different. The UCRT is a Windows system component. The Visual C++ Redistributable installs the C++ runtime on top of UCRT. On older Windows, installing the VC++ Redistributable also installs the UCRT.

title: Application Error - Event ID 1000
shortDescription: An application crashed due to an unhandled exception, logged as Event ID 1000 in the Application event log.
fullDescription:
Event ID 1000 from the Windows Error Reporting source indicates that an application has crashed due to a fault in the application itself or one of its loaded modules (DLLs). The event details include the faulting application name, faulting module, exception code, and fault offset.

This is one of the most common Event Viewer entries and can be caused by virtually any application bug, missing dependency, memory corruption, or compatibility issue. The exception code and faulting module provide crucial diagnostic information.

Common exception codes include 0xC0000005 (access violation), 0xC0000374 (heap corruption), and 0x40000015 (application termination). The faulting module name reveals which DLL caused the crash.
symptoms: Application crashes without a clear error message
Event ID 1000 entries in Event Viewer Application log
Repeated crashes of the same application
Windows Error Reporting dialog appears after crash

fixSteps.title: Analyze the Event Viewer entry
fixSteps.instruction: Open Event Viewer > Windows Logs > Application. Find Event ID 1000 entries. Note the Faulting application name, Faulting module name, and Exception code.

fixSteps.title: Update the crashing application
fixSteps.instruction: Install the latest version of the application identified in the faulting application field.

fixSteps.title: Update the faulting module
fixSteps.instruction: If the faulting module is a system DLL (ntdll.dll, kernelbase.dll), run sfc /scannow. If it is a third-party DLL, update the software that installed it.

fixSteps.title: Test memory with Windows Memory Diagnostic
fixSteps.instruction: If exception code is 0xC0000005 or 0xC0000374 and multiple apps crash, run mdsched.exe and restart to test RAM.

whenToCallPro: If multiple different applications crash with Event ID 1000, this may indicate a systemic issue like bad RAM or OS corruption requiring professional diagnosis.

faq.q: What does the faulting module tell me?
faq.a: The faulting module is the DLL where the crash occurred. If it is the application's own DLL, the app has a bug. If it is a system DLL like ntdll.dll, there may be a deeper system issue.

faq.q: What does exception code 0xC0000005 mean?
faq.a: 0xC0000005 is an access violation - the application tried to read or write memory it should not. This is the most common crash code.

faq.q: Should I be worried about occasional Event ID 1000 entries?
faq.a: Occasional single entries are normal - applications crash sometimes. Worry if you see repeated crashes of the same application or crashes across many different applications.

title: Service Terminated Unexpectedly - Event ID 7031
shortDescription: A Windows service terminated unexpectedly and the Service Control Manager is attempting recovery actions.
fullDescription:
Event ID 7031 from the Service Control Manager indicates that a service process terminated without properly stopping through the service control interface. The SCM then takes the configured recovery action, which may include restarting the service, running a program, or restarting the computer.

This error can affect any Windows service, from built-in system services to third-party services installed by applications. Common affected services include Windows Update, Print Spooler, Audio Service, and third-party antivirus or backup services.

Frequent service crashes may indicate corrupted service files, insufficient system resources, permission issues, or conflicts with other software.
symptoms: Event ID 7031 in System event log
Service-dependent functionality stops working intermittently
Service restarts automatically several times
Notification that a service stopped unexpectedly

fixSteps.title: Identify the failing service
fixSteps.instruction: Open Event Viewer > Windows Logs > System. Find Event ID 7031 and note the service name mentioned in the description.

fixSteps.title: Restart the service manually
fixSteps.instruction: Open services.msc, find the identified service, right-click > Restart. Check if it stays running.

fixSteps.title: Repair system files
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: sfc /scannow followed by DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth. Restart.

fixSteps.title: Check service dependencies
fixSteps.instruction: In services.msc, double-click the failing service > Dependencies tab. Ensure all dependent services are running.

whenToCallPro: If a critical system service keeps crashing despite repairs, or if the failing service is part of a business application.

faq.q: Is this dangerous?
faq.a: It depends on which service is failing. Critical services like Windows Update or Security Center failing may leave your system vulnerable. Non-essential services failing is less concerning.

faq.q: What are recovery actions?
faq.a: In services.msc > service Properties > Recovery tab, you can configure what happens on first, second, and subsequent failures: restart the service, run a program, or restart the computer.

faq.q: Can third-party antivirus cause this?
faq.a: Yes. Aggressive security software may interfere with service processes. Try temporarily disabling third-party antivirus to see if the service stabilizes.

title: Unexpected System Shutdown - Event ID 6008
shortDescription: The previous system shutdown was unexpected, indicating a crash, power loss, or forced restart.
fullDescription:
Event ID 6008 from EventLog records that the previous system shutdown was not clean. This event is logged when Windows boots up and detects that the previous session ended abnormally - due to a power failure, BSOD, system freeze requiring a hard reset, or kernel crash.

The event includes the date and time of the unexpected shutdown, which can help correlate with other events. Checking events around that timestamp in System and Application logs often reveals the root cause (BSOD codes, driver crashes, hardware errors).

Occasional Event 6008 entries from known power outages are harmless. Frequent entries suggest hardware problems (PSU, RAM, overheating), driver issues, or software conflicts causing system instability.

causes.cause: Power outage or accidental power disconnect
System freeze requiring hard reset (power button)
CPU or GPU overheating causing emergency shutdown

symptoms: Event ID 6008 in System event log after boot
Computer was found off unexpectedly
System rebooted without warning
BIOS shows 'improper shutdown' warning on some systems

fixSteps.title: Check for correlated events
fixSteps.instruction: In Event Viewer > System log, check events immediately before the timestamp in Event 6008. Look for BugCheck (BSOD), Kernel-Power (Event 41), or driver errors.

fixSteps.title: Check for overheating
fixSteps.instruction: Install HWMonitor or Core Temp and monitor CPU and GPU temperatures under load. Temperatures above 90°C for CPU or 85°C for GPU may cause shutdown.

fixSteps.title: Test power supply
fixSteps.instruction: For desktops: if shutdowns occur under load, the PSU may be failing. Try a different PSU or use a PSU tester. For laptops: test with the charger plugged in.

fixSteps.title: Run memory diagnostics
fixSteps.instruction: Press Win+R, type mdsched.exe, restart to test. Bad RAM can cause random crashes and unexpected shutdowns.

whenToCallPro: If unexpected shutdowns occur frequently with no clear software cause, professional hardware diagnosis of PSU, motherboard, and RAM is recommended.

faq.q: Is a single Event 6008 concerning?
faq.a: Not usually. A single occurrence after a known power outage or accidental shutdown is normal. Concern arises when these events occur frequently without explanation.

faq.q: How do I find the cause of the unexpected shutdown?
faq.a: Look at events timestamped just before the Event 6008 time. BugCheck events indicate BSOD, Kernel-Power Event 41 indicates a sudden loss of power, and disk errors indicate storage problems.

faq.q: Can software cause unexpected shutdowns?
faq.a: Yes. Driver bugs causing BSOD, malware, and software that overwrites critical system memory can all cause crashes that result in Event 6008.

title: COM Class Not Registered (REGDB_E_CLASSNOTREG)
shortDescription: A COM class required by the application is not registered on this system.
fullDescription:
Error 0x80040154 (REGDB_E_CLASSNOTREG) occurs when an application tries to create a COM (Component Object Model) object whose class is not registered in the Windows Registry. COM is a fundamental Windows technology used for inter-process communication and component reuse.

This error typically appears when a required component was not properly installed, its registration was lost due to registry corruption, or there is a 32-bit/64-bit mismatch in COM registration. It is common after incomplete software installations, failed uninstallations, or registry cleaning.

Resolution involves reinstalling the software that provides the missing COM component, re-registering DLLs, or fixing the 32/64-bit registration mismatch.

causes.cause: COM registration lost after registry cleanup

symptoms: Error 0x80040154 when launching an application
Class not registered error in application or Event Viewer
Application feature fails with COM error
Error after uninstalling related software

fixSteps.title: Reinstall the affected application
fixSteps.instruction: Uninstall the application showing the error, restart, then reinstall. The installer should re-register all required COM components.

fixSteps.title: Re-register COM DLLs
fixSteps.instruction: If you know the missing DLL, open elevated Command Prompt: regsvr32 filename.dll. For 32-bit DLLs on 64-bit Windows: C:\Windows\SysWOW64\regsvr32.exe filename.dll

fixSteps.title: Repair Microsoft Office (if Office-related)
fixSteps.instruction: Many COM errors relate to Office components. Open Settings > Apps > Microsoft Office > Modify > Online Repair.

fixSteps.title: Check component services
fixSteps.instruction: Run dcomcnfg. Navigate to Component Services > Computers > My Computer > DCOM Config. Check if the required component is listed and properly configured.

whenToCallPro: If COM registration issues affect multiple applications and standard reinstallation does not help, the Windows registry may need professional repair.

faq.q: What is COM?
faq.a: Component Object Model is a Microsoft technology that allows software components to communicate. Many Windows features and applications rely on COM for inter-process communication.

faq.q: Can registry cleaners cause this?
faq.a: Yes. Registry cleaners often remove COM registrations they consider orphaned. This is why Microsoft and most experts advise against using registry cleaners.

faq.q: What is the 32/64-bit mismatch issue?
faq.a: A 32-bit application can only use COM components registered in the 32-bit registry (WOW6432Node). If a component is only registered in the 64-bit registry, 32-bit apps cannot find it.

title: Access Denied (E_ACCESSDENIED)
shortDescription: The operation failed because the user or process does not have the required permissions.
fullDescription:
Error 0x80070005 (E_ACCESSDENIED) is a generic Windows error indicating that a process was denied access to a resource it needs. This maps to the Win32 error code ERROR_ACCESS_DENIED (5). It appears in many contexts: Windows Update, file operations, COM/DCOM access, registry modifications, and service operations.

The most common scenarios include Windows Update failing because the update service cannot access required folders, applications failing because they lack write permissions, and COM components being blocked by DCOM security settings.

Resolution depends on context but generally involves running operations as Administrator, resetting permissions on affected folders, or adjusting DCOM security settings.

causes.cause: Application not running with required administrator privileges
File or folder permissions changed or corrupted
DCOM security settings blocking COM access

symptoms: Windows Update fails with error 0x80070005
Application shows Access Denied error
Cannot modify files or registry keys
COM operations fail with E_ACCESSDENIED

fixSteps.title: Run as Administrator
fixSteps.instruction: Right-click the application or Command Prompt and select Run as administrator. Many access denied errors are simply due to insufficient privileges.

fixSteps.title: Reset Windows Update permissions
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: net stop wuauserv && net stop cryptSvc && ren C:\Windows\SoftwareDistribution SoftwareDistribution.old && net start wuauserv && net start cryptSvc

fixSteps.title: Take ownership of files
fixSteps.instruction: Right-click the file/folder > Properties > Security > Advanced > Change owner to your account. Check 'Replace all child object permissions'.

fixSteps.title: Run Windows Update Troubleshooter
fixSteps.instruction: Settings > System > Troubleshoot > Other troubleshooters > Windows Update > Run. This automatically fixes common permission issues.

whenToCallPro: If access denied errors affect system services and administrative access does not resolve the issue, the system's security configuration may need professional repair.

faq.q: Why do I get Access Denied as an administrator?
faq.a: UAC (User Account Control) means even admin accounts run with standard privileges by default. You must explicitly elevate by right-clicking > Run as administrator.

faq.q: Is it safe to take ownership of system files?
faq.a: Be cautious. Taking ownership of Windows system folders can cause security issues. Only take ownership of specific files you need to access, not entire system directories.

faq.q: Can antivirus cause access denied errors?
faq.a: Yes. Security software may block write access to files it considers protected. Try temporarily disabling antivirus to see if it is the cause.

title: File or Directory Is Corrupted and Unreadable
shortDescription: A file or directory is corrupted and unreadable, commonly occurring during Windows installation or file copy operations.
fullDescription:
Error 0x80070570 (ERROR_FILE_CORRUPT) indicates that Windows encountered a corrupted file or directory. This error appears most frequently during Windows installation (when setup files on the USB drive are corrupted), during Windows Update, or when copying files from a damaged storage device.

The corruption can be in the source media (installation USB, DVD, download), in RAM (causing data corruption during transfer), or on the destination storage device. It is one of the most common errors during fresh Windows installations.

Resolution involves recreating the installation media, checking RAM integrity, and scanning the disk for errors.

causes.cause: Corrupted Windows installation USB or ISO
Faulty RAM causing data corruption during copy
Failing storage device with bad sectors

symptoms: Error 0x80070570 during Windows installation
File copy operations fail with corruption error
Windows Update fails with this error code
Cannot extract or access certain files

fixSteps.title: Recreate installation media
fixSteps.instruction: If error occurs during Windows install: download a fresh Windows ISO using the Media Creation Tool and create a new USB drive. Use a different USB drive if possible.

fixSteps.title: Test RAM
fixSteps.instruction: Run Windows Memory Diagnostic (mdsched.exe) or MemTest86 from USB. Bad RAM is a very common cause of file corruption errors. Run for at least 4 passes.

fixSteps.title: Clear Windows Update cache
fixSteps.instruction: For Windows Update errors: open elevated CMD: net stop wuauserv && rd /s /q C:\Windows\SoftwareDistribution && net start wuauserv

fixSteps.title: Run CHKDSK on destination drive
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: chkdsk C: /f /r. Schedule it for next restart. This repairs bad sectors on the target drive.

whenToCallPro: If RAM tests reveal errors (requiring RAM replacement) or if the drive has significant bad sectors indicating imminent failure.

faq.q: Why does this happen during Windows installation?
faq.a: The most common causes are a corrupt USB drive or bad RAM. USB drives can have silent errors, and RAM faults corrupt data during the large file transfers that installation involves.

faq.q: Can I just retry the installation?
faq.a: You can try, and sometimes it works if the corruption was transient. But if it fails again, recreate the USB and test your RAM.

faq.q: Does this mean my hard drive is failing?
faq.a: Not necessarily. The corruption is often in the source (USB) or in RAM during transfer. Run CHKDSK on the target drive to check its health.

title: S.M.A.R.T. Disk Health Warning
shortDescription: Your hard drive or SSD is reporting a S.M.A.R.T. warning indicating potential hardware failure.
fullDescription:
S.M.A.R.T. (Self-Monitoring, Analysis, and Reporting Technology) is a monitoring system built into hard drives and SSDs. When a drive detects that key health parameters have degraded beyond manufacturer thresholds, it reports a warning. These warnings should be taken seriously as they often precede complete drive failure.

Common S.M.A.R.T. warnings include Reallocated Sector Count (bad sectors being remapped), Current Pending Sector Count (sectors waiting to be remapped), Spin Retry Count (HDD motor struggling), and Wear Leveling Count (SSD cell wear). Some warnings are urgent (indicating imminent failure) while others indicate gradual degradation.

The primary response to a S.M.A.R.T. warning is to immediately back up all important data and plan for drive replacement.

causes.cause: Normal wear and aging of drive
Excessive read/write operations wearing out SSD cells
Physical shock or vibration damaging HDD platters
Power supply issues causing improper shutdowns

symptoms: Windows or BIOS displays S.M.A.R.T. warning at boot
CrystalDiskInfo shows Caution or Bad status
Increasing bad sectors over time
Occasional file read errors or slow file access
Clicking or grinding sounds from HDD

fixSteps.title: Back up data immediately
fixSteps.instruction: Before doing anything else, back up all important data to an external drive or cloud storage. S.M.A.R.T. warnings can precede sudden failure.

fixSteps.title: Check S.M.A.R.T. details
fixSteps.instruction: Install CrystalDiskInfo and check the detailed S.M.A.R.T. attributes. Pay attention to Reallocated Sector Count, Current Pending Sector, and Uncorrectable Sector Count.

fixSteps.title: Run manufacturer's diagnostic tool
fixSteps.instruction: Download the drive manufacturer's diagnostic tool (Seagate SeaTools, WD Data Lifeguard, Samsung Magician, etc.) and run a full test.

fixSteps.title: Plan drive replacement
fixSteps.instruction: Order a replacement drive. Clone the current drive to the new one using Macrium Reflect or Clonezilla while it still works.

whenToCallPro: If you need to recover data from a drive that has already failed, professional data recovery services are necessary and can be expensive ($300-$2000+).

faq.q: How long do I have after a S.M.A.R.T. warning?
faq.a: There is no reliable prediction. Some drives fail within days, others last months or years. The safest approach is to back up immediately and replace the drive soon.

faq.q: Can S.M.A.R.T. warnings be false alarms?
faq.a: Rarely. S.M.A.R.T. is based on actual physical measurements. While a single reallocated sector on a new drive may not be concerning, a warning threshold being crossed is significant.

faq.q: Should I keep using a drive with a S.M.A.R.T. warning?
faq.a: Only if you have a complete backup and can tolerate sudden failure. Do not store important data only on a drive with S.M.A.R.T. warnings.

title: Application Unable to Start - Bad Image Format
shortDescription: The application was unable to start correctly (0xC000007B), typically due to a 32-bit/64-bit mismatch or corrupted runtime.
fullDescription:
Error 0xC000007B (STATUS_INVALID_IMAGE_FORMAT) when launching applications is distinct from the BSOD variant. This runtime error means the application or one of its DLL dependencies has an invalid format. The most common cause is a 64-bit application trying to load a 32-bit DLL or vice versa.

This error is extremely common with games and applications that require specific Visual C++ Redistributable or DirectX versions. It also occurs when system DLLs in the System32 or SysWOW64 folders are corrupted or replaced with incorrect versions.

The fix typically involves installing all Visual C++ Redistributable versions, reinstalling DirectX, and ensuring the correct architecture versions of all DLLs are present.

causes.cause: Missing or wrong-architecture Visual C++ Redistributable

symptoms: Application was unable to start correctly (0xc000007b)
Error appears immediately when launching the application
Game launches briefly then crashes with this error
Error started after Windows update or system change

fixSteps.title: Install all Visual C++ Redistributables
fixSteps.instruction: Install all Visual C++ Redistributable packages from 2005 through 2022, both x86 and x64 versions. Download from Microsoft's official site.

fixSteps.title: Install DirectX End-User Runtime
fixSteps.instruction: Download and run the DirectX End-User Runtime Web Installer from Microsoft to install all legacy DirectX components.

fixSteps.title: Install .NET Framework 3.5
fixSteps.instruction: Enable .NET Framework 3.5 via Windows Features (Control Panel > Programs > Turn Windows features on or off).

fixSteps.title: Run SFC and DISM
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth && sfc /scannow. This repairs corrupted system DLLs.

whenToCallPro: If installing all redistributables and runtime packages does not fix the issue, the application installation itself may be corrupted.

faq.q: Why do I need so many Visual C++ versions?
faq.a: Different applications are compiled with different Visual Studio versions. Each version needs its own redistributable. Installing all of them ensures maximum compatibility.

faq.q: Should I install x86 versions on 64-bit Windows?
faq.a: Yes. Many applications and games are still 32-bit and need the x86 (32-bit) runtime libraries, even on 64-bit Windows.

faq.q: Can I use a DLL fixer tool?
faq.a: Avoid third-party DLL fixer tools. They often install incorrect DLL versions or bundle malware. Use the official Microsoft redistributable packages instead.

title: Kernel-Power Critical Error - Unexpected Restart
shortDescription: The system rebooted without cleanly shutting down first, often caused by power loss, hardware failure, or critical kernel error.
fullDescription:
Event ID 41 from the Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-Power source is a critical event indicating that the system restarted without a clean shutdown. This is distinct from BSOD events because no crash dump is created - the system simply lost power or stopped responding completely.

The BugcheckCode field in the event details helps identify the cause. A BugcheckCode of 0 typically means a complete power loss (PSU failure, power outage, or the power button was held). Non-zero codes indicate a kernel crash that was so severe the system could not write a crash dump.

Frequent Kernel-Power Event 41 entries are a significant indicator of hardware problems, particularly with the PSU, RAM, or motherboard. Software causes include unstable overclocks and driver bugs that cause complete system hangs.

causes.cause: Driver bug causing complete system hang

symptoms: Computer reboots or shuts down without warning
Event ID 41 Kernel-Power in System event log
No BSOD screen before the restart
Issue occurs under heavy load (gaming, rendering)
Random reboots at unpredictable intervals

fixSteps.title: Check the BugcheckCode
fixSteps.instruction: In Event Viewer > System log, find Event 41. Check the BugcheckCode in Details tab. Code 0 = power loss. Other codes = kernel crash.

fixSteps.title: Remove all overclocks
fixSteps.instruction: Reset BIOS to default settings to remove any CPU, RAM, or GPU overclocks. Test stability for several days.

fixSteps.title: Test RAM extensively
fixSteps.instruction: Run MemTest86 from USB for at least 8 passes. Even 1 error means the RAM stick is faulty and should be replaced.

fixSteps.title: Monitor temperatures and voltages
fixSteps.instruction: Install HWiNFO64 and monitor CPU temp, GPU temp, and PSU voltages (12V, 5V, 3.3V) under load. Voltages should not fluctuate more than 5%.

whenToCallPro: If random reboots persist after removing overclocks and testing RAM, professional PSU and motherboard testing is recommended.

faq.q: What does BugcheckCode 0 mean?
faq.a: BugcheckCode 0 means there was no crash dump, indicating a sudden total power loss. This points to PSU failure, power outage, or the user holding the power button.

faq.q: Why does this only happen during gaming?
faq.a: Gaming puts maximum load on both CPU and GPU simultaneously, which draws the most power. A PSU that cannot handle peak load will shut down or become unstable.

faq.q: Can Windows Update cause this?
faq.a: In rare cases, a driver delivered through Windows Update can cause kernel hangs. If the issue started right after an update, roll back the driver through Device Manager.

title: CHKDSK Found Bad Sectors on Disk
shortDescription: CHKDSK has detected bad sectors on the disk, indicating potential storage device degradation.
fullDescription:
When CHKDSK (Check Disk) reports bad sectors, it means portions of the disk surface can no longer reliably store data. For HDDs, bad sectors are physical damage to the magnetic platter surface. For SSDs, they represent worn-out NAND flash cells that have exceeded their write endurance.

CHKDSK with the /r flag attempts to recover readable data from bad sectors and marks them as unusable so the file system avoids them. A small number of bad sectors on an older drive may be normal wear, but a rapidly growing number indicates the drive is failing.

The key indicator is whether bad sectors are increasing over time. Run CHKDSK periodically and compare results. If the count grows, plan for drive replacement.

causes.cause: Normal aging and wear of storage media
SSD NAND cell wear from excessive writes

symptoms: CHKDSK reports bad sectors in output
Files become corrupted or unreadable
Windows runs CHKDSK automatically at boot
Slow file access on certain areas of the disk
Blue screen errors related to disk I/O

fixSteps.title: Run CHKDSK with repair
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: chkdsk C: /f /r. Schedule for next restart. The /r flag recovers data from bad sectors and marks them.

fixSteps.title: Check S.M.A.R.T. data
fixSteps.instruction: Install CrystalDiskInfo and check Reallocated Sector Count and Current Pending Sector Count. Note the raw values for tracking.

fixSteps.title: Back up important data
fixSteps.instruction: Immediately copy important files to another drive. Use robocopy for large transfers: robocopy D:\Data E:\Backup /E /R:1 /W:1 (retries once, waits 1 second on error).

fixSteps.title: Monitor and plan replacement
fixSteps.instruction: Run CHKDSK and CrystalDiskInfo weekly. If bad sectors are increasing, replace the drive. Clone using Macrium Reflect while the drive still works.

whenToCallPro: If bad sectors are multiplying rapidly or if the drive contains critical data you cannot afford to lose, professional data recovery should be considered before the drive fails completely.

faq.q: Can CHKDSK fix bad sectors permanently?
faq.a: No. CHKDSK marks bad sectors so they are not used, and recovers data if possible. The physical damage remains. On SSDs, worn cells cannot be restored.

faq.q: How many bad sectors are too many?
faq.a: Any growing number is concerning. A handful of stable bad sectors on an old HDD may be acceptable if they do not increase. But new bad sectors appearing regularly means the drive is failing.

faq.q: Should I defragment a drive with bad sectors?
faq.a: For HDDs, defragmentation moves data around and may expose more bad areas. For SSDs, never defragment. Focus on backing up and replacing the drive instead.

title: Distributed COM (DCOM) Communication Error
shortDescription: A Distributed COM operation failed due to permission, network, or configuration issues between COM components.
fullDescription:
DCOM (Distributed Component Object Model) errors occur when COM components that communicate across process or network boundaries encounter failures. These errors are logged extensively in Event Viewer and can relate to security permissions (DCOM Event 10016), network connectivity, or misconfigured COM components.

DCOM is used heavily by Windows itself and by many enterprise applications. Common DCOM errors include permission denied for launching or activating COM servers, timeouts waiting for COM server responses, and class not registered errors for remote components.

Many DCOM errors in Event Viewer are harmless and caused by known Windows component permission mismatches. However, DCOM errors that affect application functionality require investigation and resolution.
symptoms: DCOM errors flooding Event Viewer
Event ID 10016 with permission denied
Remote COM operations failing
Applications that use COM interop crashing

fixSteps.title: Identify the problematic CLSID
fixSteps.instruction: In Event Viewer, find the DCOM error and note the CLSID and APPID values. These identify the specific COM component.

fixSteps.title: Fix DCOM permissions
fixSteps.instruction: Run dcomcnfg > Component Services > Computers > My Computer > DCOM Config. Find the component by APPID, right-click > Properties > Security. Add the affected user/group with proper permissions.

fixSteps.title: Take ownership of registry key
fixSteps.instruction: Open regedit, navigate to HKCR\CLSID\{the-CLSID}. Right-click > Permissions > Advanced > Change owner. Then grant your user Full Control.

fixSteps.title: Ignore known benign DCOM errors
fixSteps.instruction: Some DCOM 10016 errors for system components (like RuntimeBroker, ShellServiceHost) are known Windows issues with no functional impact. Microsoft recommends ignoring them.

whenToCallPro: If DCOM errors cause actual application failures (not just Event Viewer noise), and the DCOM configuration is complex, professional IT support is recommended.

faq.q: Are all DCOM errors in Event Viewer harmful?
faq.a: No. Many DCOM 10016 errors are known Windows issues that have no functional impact. Microsoft has acknowledged these as benign in many cases.

faq.q: What is the difference between COM and DCOM?
faq.a: COM is for communication between components on the same machine. DCOM extends COM to work across networks. Both use the same registration and security infrastructure.

faq.q: Can I disable DCOM?
faq.a: Disabling DCOM will break many Windows features and applications. It is not recommended. Instead, fix the specific permissions or components causing errors.

title: Windows Error Reporting Application Crash
shortDescription: An application crashed and Windows Error Reporting captured the fault details.
fullDescription:
APPCRASH is a Windows Error Reporting (WER) event name that appears when an unmanaged (native C/C++) application crashes. The WER dialog shows the faulting application, faulting module, and exception code. This is the native equivalent of CLR20r3 for .NET applications.

The most useful diagnostic information in an APPCRASH event is the faulting module name and exception code. Common exception codes include 0xC0000005 (access violation), 0xC0000409 (stack buffer overrun detected), and 0xC0000096 (privileged instruction). The faulting module reveals which DLL or EXE component caused the crash.

Resolution depends on whether the fault is in the application itself, a system DLL, or a third-party component like a driver or security software hook.

causes.cause: Security software hooking DLLs and causing conflicts

symptoms: Application crashes with APPCRASH dialog
Problem Event Name: APPCRASH in error details
Application repeatedly crashes at the same point
Crash occurs after system or application update

fixSteps.title: Note the faulting module
fixSteps.instruction: When the APPCRASH dialog appears, click 'Show problem details' and note the Faulting Module Name and Exception Code. This identifies the root cause.

fixSteps.title: Update the application
fixSteps.instruction: Download and install the latest version of the crashing application. Developers frequently fix crash bugs in updates.

fixSteps.title: Check for DLL conflicts
fixSteps.instruction: If the faulting module is not the application itself, identify what software installed it. Update or remove that software.

fixSteps.title: Run in compatibility mode
fixSteps.instruction: Right-click the application > Properties > Compatibility tab. Try running in compatibility mode for an earlier Windows version.

whenToCallPro: If the crashing application is business-critical and cannot be updated or replaced, contact the software vendor for a fix.

faq.q: What is WER?
faq.a: Windows Error Reporting is the system that captures crash data, displays the error dialog, and optionally sends crash reports to Microsoft or the application vendor.

faq.q: Should I send the crash report?
faq.a: Yes, if the option is available. Crash reports help developers identify and fix bugs. No personal data is typically included in crash reports.

faq.q: Can antivirus cause APPCRASH?
faq.a: Yes. Some antivirus products inject DLLs into all processes for monitoring. These hooks can conflict with certain applications and cause crashes.

title: ntdll.dll Application Crash
shortDescription: An application crashed with ntdll.dll as the faulting module, indicating a low-level system call failure.
fullDescription:
When ntdll.dll appears as the faulting module in a crash report, it means the crash occurred in the Windows NT Layer DLL, which is the lowest user-mode layer above the kernel. Almost all Windows API calls eventually go through ntdll.dll, so seeing it as the faulting module does not mean ntdll.dll itself is broken.

Instead, ntdll.dll crashes typically indicate that the application passed invalid data to a system call, has a heap corruption bug, or that memory was corrupted by faulty RAM or another component. ntdll.dll is simply where the corruption is detected.

Common exception codes with ntdll.dll crashes include 0xC0000005 (access violation during a system call), 0xC0000374 (heap corruption detected), and 0xC0000409 (stack buffer overrun detected by security cookie check).

causes.cause: Application heap corruption detected by ntdll

symptoms: Application crash with ntdll.dll as faulting module
Multiple different applications crash citing ntdll.dll
Exception code 0xC0000374 (heap corruption)
Crashes are random and not consistently reproducible

fixSteps.title: Update the crashing application
fixSteps.instruction: Install the latest version of the application that crashes. Heap corruption bugs are often fixed in updates.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC to verify ntdll.dll
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: sfc /scannow. While ntdll.dll corruption is rare, SFC will detect and repair it.

fixSteps.title: Test RAM
fixSteps.instruction: If multiple applications crash with ntdll.dll, run Windows Memory Diagnostic (mdsched.exe) or MemTest86. Bad RAM causes random heap corruption.

fixSteps.title: Perform clean boot
fixSteps.instruction: Run msconfig > Services > Hide all Microsoft services > Disable all. Startup tab > Disable all. Restart. If crashes stop, re-enable services in groups to find the culprit.

whenToCallPro: If multiple applications crash with ntdll.dll heap corruption and RAM tests are clean, the Windows installation may be damaged and require professional repair.

faq.q: Is ntdll.dll corrupted?
faq.a: Almost certainly not. ntdll.dll is a protected system file that is very rarely corrupted. It appears in crash reports because it is where the application's bad behavior is detected.

faq.q: Why do different applications crash in ntdll.dll?
faq.a: If multiple different applications crash in ntdll.dll, the problem is likely systemic: bad RAM, a misbehaving system-wide DLL hook, or corrupted Windows components.

faq.q: What is heap corruption?
faq.a: The heap is a memory area where applications store dynamically allocated data. Corruption means something wrote data to the wrong memory location, damaging data structures that ntdll.dll manages.

title: Windows Virtual Memory / Page File Error
shortDescription: Windows is running low on virtual memory or the page file has encountered an error.
fullDescription:
Windows virtual memory errors occur when the page file (pagefile.sys) is too small, disabled, or corrupted. The page file extends physical RAM by using disk space as overflow memory. When it runs out or cannot be accessed, applications and Windows itself may become unstable.

Common symptoms include the warning 'Your system is low on virtual memory', application crashes with out-of-memory errors despite having free RAM, and system sluggishness when many applications are open. The page file also provides the backing store for crash dumps, so a missing page file means no BSOD dump files.

The default Windows behavior is to automatically manage the page file size, which works well for most users. Problems arise when the page file is manually set too small, placed on a full drive, or disabled entirely.

causes.cause: Page file manually set too small or disabled
System drive running out of space
Memory-hungry applications exceeding available virtual memory
Page file on a failing drive

symptoms: Warning: Your system is low on virtual memory
Applications crash with out-of-memory errors
System becomes very slow when using many applications
No crash dump files generated after BSOD

fixSteps.title: Set page file to automatic
fixSteps.instruction: Right-click This PC > Properties > Advanced system settings > Performance Settings > Advanced > Virtual Memory > Change. Check 'Automatically manage paging file size for all drives'.

fixSteps.title: Free disk space
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure the system drive has at least 10GB free. Run Disk Cleanup (cleanmgr) and delete temporary files, old Windows updates, and other unnecessary files.

fixSteps.title: Set custom page file size
fixSteps.instruction: If automatic is not enough: set Initial Size to 1.5x your RAM and Maximum Size to 3x your RAM. For 16GB RAM: Initial=24576 MB, Maximum=49152 MB.

fixSteps.title: Move page file to another drive
fixSteps.instruction: If the system drive is an SSD with limited space, add a second page file on another drive: Virtual Memory > select the drive > Custom size.

whenToCallPro: If virtual memory errors persist despite correct page file settings and adequate disk space, the system may need more physical RAM or there may be a memory leak.

faq.q: Should I disable the page file?
faq.a: No. Even with abundant RAM, Windows uses the page file for memory-mapped files, crash dumps, and memory management. Disabling it can cause application crashes and prevents BSOD dump creation.

faq.q: Does the page file wear out my SSD?
faq.a: Modern SSDs have sufficient write endurance to handle page file writes for many years. The performance benefit of an SSD page file outweighs the minimal wear.

faq.q: How much page file do I need?
faq.a: For most users, letting Windows manage it automatically is best. If you set it manually, 1.5x to 2x your RAM size is a good starting point.
